speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, John.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Mrs. Gabriela Katz, maiden name, Spinner, on May 10, 1996, in San Diego, California. My name's John Kent. And we're doing this in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, John. Getting up to steam and go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Darlene Vicki Katz Jacobs. And she's today with me to find out a little bit more about the war times. And that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one thing I wanted to do was mention a couple of things about my dad, who died four years ago. But he would have been very interested in--

speaker: interviewee
text: The project.

speaker: interviewee
text: --This whole project. A couple of stories that he told me a few times, when I became interested-- when I was in high school-- in my parents past, I'd like to tell you. One story-- my dad was a doctor. And he was in-- I don't know which camp it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the one way where he got-- that the entire camp was infected.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, this was camp in Poland. The name was [POLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And he didn't-- from what he said, he didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: 90% Of the people were sick with typhoid.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And he said that he was a doctor, although he kept that a secret. And there was a camp doctor, who knew that my dad was a doctor and told him, we have this problem. I don't know what these people have. I don't know what the illness is, so can you come and help me? And my dad saw right away that it was typhoid fever. And I guess what my dad said would have happened was if the Nazis knew that there was typhoid fever in the camp, they would have all been exterminated right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they hatched a plan to tell the Germans that it was a different kind of disease. I don't know what it was  called.

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's not contagious, you see. In this way, he saved everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And then my dad continued to treat all these people and ended up saving them because of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Saving a few of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other story that my dad told me was I think he wanted to give me the idea that there came a point where the prisoners didn't care if they were dead or alive. They realized that they were going to be killed at any time. And so life didn't mean very much. And he said that at this time, he was working by chopping down trees. And there was a Nazi who would come and just start beating prisoners at will. And when he would beat them to the point where they were not useful anymore, than they would be shot. And so he said that this Nazi came and started beating him. And my father, knowing that he was going to die if he continued to be beaten, he picked up his axe. And he said, I will kill you, if you continue to beat me. And I guess the look in his face-- my father didn't think that-- he didn't think that he would survive this. And he figured that he might as well just stop the beating. Let this man kill him, and that would be the end. But I guess he scared him. And the Nazi said, go back to your room or your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Work.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Your work or whatever. No, he told him to go back to the cabin, wherever he stayed. And my dad was walking towards this place. And he felt that he would be shot any minute. He heard him take a gun out. And he said that he walked very slowly, intentionally, walked all the way there and then walked inside and fainted. But this person never shot him. And my reaction to the story was, you were so brave. This is so amazing that you did this. And he said, I wasn't brave. I just knew I was going to die one way or another. And it just didn't make a difference anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that story really let me know that even though to me he was being brave, to him it was, in a sense, a way out. You know? He was going to die either way.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a working camp, you see. The people, the Jewish people who were lended by Gestapo to the firm that was cutting the trees. German firm, you know? And this was a working camp, but very, very tough situation, was that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing that always has amazed me about this whole time period in my parents' life is the way they treated each other during that time. The way my mother gave things up for my father. My father gave things up for my mother. They always managed, one way or another, to either know where they were or be able to find each other, get into the same camp. The way my mother got on Schindler's list-- my mother wasn't on Schindler's list. And my dad, when they were reading the names of people to get from one line, which was going to be exterminated, into the other line, which were people who were going to Schindler's camp, my dad yelled out my mother's name. And she walked over into that line. And they didn't know, actually, that they were going to be saved. They just knew that they wanted to be in the same line together. And so they picked the right line. And I think those instances of just taking a chance and knowing what to do at the right time. And at the same time, being very moral about it, they didn't do things to other people that were horrible, in order to save themselves. And I think that they instilled in me a sense of morality. They instilled in my sister a sense of morality, that really, we're very grateful for. They also instilled in us a sense of being very special, that we are very special. They never said because they were survivors. But we feel ourselves that we are the only people in our family, that we know of, who have survived. And that's a very special feeling. And we better do something really great with our lives.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And a sense that my parents have always said that one reason they survived is because they were very educated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And young, very young.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they weren't really skinny, so they could last for a long time. I remember hearing that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they were educated. Both my sister and I are very educated. That's one thing my mother always told me. I don't care what you do with your life. But you will be educated. And I will do that for you. And these are things that I think came from that experience somewhat. I think that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Juda Katz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Moses Juda Katz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Juda Moses Katz. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm getting back up to speed. And I've lost my pen. Can you stop this for a second? OK, getting up to speed. And go ahead, John.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's start with spelling your name please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gabriela Katz, born Spinner. You want me to spell everything, or just Spinner?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name, full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gabriela, G-A-B-R-I-E-L-A, Katz, K-A-T-Z, Spinner, S-P-I-N-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your birth date and current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: 17 Of March, 1919. I was born in Poland, Przemysl, P-R-Z-E-M-Y-S-L. And my current age is 77.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are the names of your parents and brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Cecilia Spandorf, S-P-A-N-D-O-R-F. And I had one brother. His name was Marcel Spinner, S-P-I-N-N-E-R. And my father's name was Naftali, N-A-F-T-A-L-I Spinner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's start out with looking at your early family life. What did your parents do for work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother played bridge. This was her work. My father was CPA. And he was managing the mill.

speaker: interviewer
text: A flour mill?

speaker: interviewee
text: A flour mill, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of standard of living did your family have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good, in Przemysl. Both kids were going to school. I finished the high school before the war. And I was two years in medical college in Poland, Przemysl. Then in '39, the war broke out. I get married, and I left Przemysl.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll spend a little more time on the early years. Describe what your town was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: My town?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, especially the Jewish community part of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very lovely town. It had a big Jewish community. Most people, they were educated. It was not a type of shtetl. Many people were intelligent, educated. And it was nice town. Of course, we were exposed to anti-Semitism. Before the war, everybody was at this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What types of examples do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when I was in Lvov, I couldn't at the-- I couldn't sit down for the lectures. Because the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH], they called this society like-- they wouldn't let me. If we had to take the names that we were attending the courses, the students couldn't get in. Because these people didn't let us in. You know, there was like pogroms in my city. As a matter of fact, there was one student which was not Jewish. But his father was a Polish hero. And he was going with me-- because I was afraid to go, in the evening-- back to there where I supposed to go. And they hit him over the head. They broke his skull. And it was a big story in our Poland. It was not pleasant to go of the university in Poland at this time. It was anti-Semitic. But not everybody was like this. The Jews had a good life. Many Jews were poor, that's true. But we couldn't do anything about it. The students could not attend, for example, the medical school. You had to have a very big push to get into the medical school, if you were Jewish. And if you finished the medical school outside of Poland, you couldn't come back to Poland and practice to be a doctor, that was out. So the Jewish students were forced to attend law schools, engineering, something like this. And still was a quota. But in medical school, it was one-- two Jewish girls, pardon me. This was me and one other Jewish girl from another city that her father was a doctor in. And after me, it was only one Jewish student. And then it was numerus nullus, nobody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was-- and like, we tried to get the education. My husband never went. He went to Italy, and he studied in Italy. But then, because my father had the pull, he came back to Poland. And then war broke. In what way was your family Jewish? Was it a religious family, or more traditional? Or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were Zionist. But my father was, you know, traditional Jew. And they were going to the temple. We had the religious education in schools. We did. And I belonged to the Jewish Zionistic Organization, [NON-ENGLISH]. And so the name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And but I was very assimilated. I believed that I was Polish. Very assimilated. But the kids were going to temple. And we observed all Holy Days. And this was it. And when my father was giving a lot of money to Israel-- as a matter of fact, a few of my family went to Israel before the war-- and this was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your personal relationships like with the Polish population growing up? I mean, you say assimilated.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was assimilated, yeah. You know, I figure out in every nationality, you have bad people and good people. And as a matter of fact, my father all the time was working with not-Jewish people. And some people, they were very influential.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know anything about how the Jewish community attempted to respond to the persecution? Was there any talk of how to deal with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, everybody was dealing with it themselves. It was not organized. But we had very famous lawyers, that they were writing the-- they went into politics, you know? And rabbis, very famous, that they are writing books about them still, like Rabbi Shmelkes, or-- I don't remember the other names. But they were very, very educated people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you also describe, personally, what your mother and father were like, and brother, as people? What are your memories of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of them? I lived with them for 19, 20 years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I adore my father. He was extremely smart guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: But my mother, I wasn't so fond about. But she was very pretty. You know? But I always was with my father. When I was little, I was sitting when he had meetings and so on. And I was very, very proud of him. And my brother, to me, was enfant terrible. You know what this mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I had a boyfriend waiting for me downstairs, under the window, he was able to put the water on his new hat, or something like this. But he was very capable. And he was a pianist. When he was nine years old, he was playing with the symphony orchestra. And then he was playing the accordion and so on. Very capable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you describe yourself as a kid and a teenager?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a teenager, nobody was like me. I was only one. You know what I mean? I was the prettiest, to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always was telling to my daughters, you think of yourself that you are the smartest and the prettiest. And this will be your strength. And this was me. Right? Great teaching. Throughout the '30s then, as things started to get worse in Germany, how much did you and your family know about Hitler and the general trend throughout the '30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little, not too much. We knew what's going on in Germany and so on. And then later, before the war, the people started to escape from this part of Poland that was next, near Germany. So they were telling.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, we always were under the impression that nothing can happen to us. My father was an Austrian officer. During the First World War, they were in Vienna. They lived in Vienna, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I cannot really put my fingers on this, how we felt about it. Then everything started to be so [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] swift, that you didn't think. You were doing the things that you thought is all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any talk of maybe leaving Poland or going to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had family that we sent to Palestine. For example, my husband's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sister, his mother, and her husband, they needed 1,000 pounds to go to Palestine this time. So my husband's father produced this, that they go. My mother's cousin, which was a physician, was one of the first to go to Israel. And he was one of the first organized Hadassah. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then started my friends, the younger generation, that belonged to these organizations, started to go to Israel. You know, friends of my husband, my friends. So the people started to go to Israel. And we were very interested in--

speaker: interviewer
text: How about you and your family? Did you ever consider leaving Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never, never before the war. You know that Hitler taught us one thing-- that if you are-- how you say-- you think you are somebody else, you are not. You are Jewish. Doesn't matter what. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You're living in the country that it's not your country, and he taught you this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would the Polish population know that you were Jewish, if you were pretty much assimilated?

speaker: interviewee
text: They always knew that you were Jewish. I don't know how. Maybe my nose. [LAUGHS] But they knew. I really tried to be not Jewish, you know. I got the papers during the war. And I was moving swiftly. And never anybody caught me. But this was not a regular case. You see, I decided that I have to play this role. I was smart enough to do it good.

speaker: interviewee
text: For example, I was in the train going from Przemysl to another town, OK? And I was going with a Polish underground [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] men that was trying to help me. We were sitting in this train. And there were two girls there, in this train, looking 100% like not a Jewish girl-- blonde, blue eyes, you know? The first thing what they ask us was are they checking the streets-- the Germans-- are they checking the streets looking for Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No Irish girl would ask those questions. They wouldn't have interest in it. So the Polish underground guy said to me, these girls don't have a chance. They right away, to be recognized. But I had a story. I came to the town, Zakopane. And they had the train, like, going up. You had to pass through the Gestapo. I had my [NON-ENGLISH] with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody was showing the documents. So I did it too. So this Gestapo guy looks at me, he looks at me and said, this document is not signed. So I said, it's not signed? I will sign it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took out the pen from his hand, and I signed it. And he looked. And he let us through. And this Polish underground was with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, you know what? This is crazy. This guy knows that you're supposed to sign it when you getting it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the best was to see that this is a false document. But you know, he said, you were so persuasive that they didn't catch you. So everything was simply mostly luck. You know, the Germans said in the camps, you go to the right, and you go to the left. You didn't know where the people were going, to the right or to the left. So the left line went to be exterminated. And the right one was sent to work. So really, you never knew, it was simply luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's start in then, with the beginning of the war. What do you remember of September of '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: September of '39, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were home. And oh, you know, this was completely unexpected. We heard the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] shooting. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] heard the planes. But you know, Poland had maybe two, three planes. And then we heard real planes, the German planes. And this is how it started. In our town, first we were under German occupation. Then the Russian came and took over. And then again, German took over. My husband and my brother, they escape. They went east.
